quotes.verbatim_quote:
When we are in one key a chord may be Gma? while still in another key — the same chord has a different name,
| Emi7. Below I have analyzed the harmonic structure of these chords. You are to learn both names of these chords
in all keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the relative-substitution principle (Gma6 = Emi7) that underlies most of Baker's later solo-over-changes thinking, requiring the student to internalize each shape under two functional identities.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Its best to write them out in your work book like this, Gma6 is also Emi7; A’ma6 is also Fmi7. Do this chromat-
ically always up the finger board with each chord, because in the next few lessons I'm using these chords, and
both names in some progressions.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the chromatic notebook drill Baker uses to fix dual-naming in muscle memory, and signals that subsequent lessons will assume the student can recognize either name on sight.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A lot of these progressions will not be diagrammed as they were before. I'll
simply write the chord and put its number over it, so it will be up to you to find them.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Marks the pedagogical pivot from spoon-fed diagrams to numbered-position recall, forcing the student to operate from the chord-number system Baker established earlier.

quotes.verbatim_quote:

7th z —Root 3rd 7 b5th

Cy 3rd —] a =_a
Root ie Sth azaee 7th 3rd Dh $s we 9th
3rd +~7th Sth— PETE oh 7th

quotes.why_load_bearing: Documents Baker's explicit annotation that G7#5 and Db9b5 are voiced with the root omitted, a load-bearing tritone-substitution insight (G7#5 ↔ Db9b5) that explains how he derives the b5-substitution voicings used throughout.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
LESSON 21
BOP BLUES PROGRESSIONS IN G MAJOR
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the bop blues reharmonization as a discrete unit of study, the template Baker uses to demonstrate dense ii-V chord motion (Gmi7 Gmi6 / F#ma7 / Bbmi7 Ami7 etc.) over a standard 12-bar form.

quotes.verbatim_quote: In this, our last lesson in chord study, I have written out some of the most used chord progressions in Jazz today. These are standard changes, and if you analyze them and work with them long enough, you will find that they appear in many different songs in various places.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the eleven cycles as the canonical harmonic skeletons of the standard repertoire, the substitution-targets every later solo lesson is meant to be played over.

quotes.verbatim_quote: There are eleven different examples and below each example I have the new changes that sound best in their places. Work them out thoroughly in the keys that they are in, then make up charts of each in all of the other keys: F, G, Ab, Bb, C, Db, and Eb. Then work them out in these new keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the exact seven-key transposition regime for the sequences, which converts pattern memorization into the cross-key fluency required for real-time substitution.

quotes.verbatim_quote: These charts will be very helpful to you in transposing from one key to another. They will also play a very important part in Lesson No. 35 and 46 (See Lesson No. 45 and 46).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Flags that the eleven sequences are not isolated exercises but the substrate for the later run-connection and solo lessons, making this chapter the harmonic spine of the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Standard:
C7
Fmi
G7
G7
G+7
C
New:
Gmi7 Gmi6
Gmi7 C7b5
Fmi7 Fmi6
Dmi7 Dmi6
Dmi7 G13b5b9
Cma7 Ebmi7
Dmi7 G13b9
quotes.why_load_bearing: Shows the characteristic Baker substitution move — replacing each standard change with a two-voicing ii-V or relative-minor pair (Gmi7 Gmi6 for C7, Dmi7 G13b9 for G7) — the template that all ten following cycles vary.
